purpose: Determines corporate earnings sustainability by decomposing aggregate profits into macro accounting components (Investment + Government Deficit + Net Exports − Household Saving + Dividends).
components: Government fiscal deficit (~$1.8-2.0T, primary driver this cycle)
Government deficit as direct profit source (~$1.4-1.6T annually at 6-7% GDP)
Net policy stance calculation (fiscal easing 300-400bp vs monetary tightening 525bp)
Break threshold at ~4.5% GDP deficit where buffer weakens
Full transmission chain: fiscal -> revenue -> EBITDA -> coverage -> OC test -> CLO -> loan demand
Historical deficit comparison across 5 credit cycle peaks (1989: 2.8%, 2006: 1.9%, current: 6-7%)
Private investment (AI capex at ~$250B as the entire growth margin)
Government fiscal deficit ($1.8-2T as level floor)
CEO capex decisions as the binding variable (4-5 decision-makers)
current_assessment: AI capex constitutes the entire growth margin of private investment. Fiscal deficit provides a floor but not growth. Macro regime is a call option on 4-5 capital allocation decisions with no diversification across decision-makers. A 25% capex cut implies $50-60B earnings impact with ±30-50% uncertainty.
limitations: Behavioral pass-through elasticities are imprecisely estimated
The 2pp consolidation threshold is a heuristic, not precisely calibrated
Does not specify the timing or composition of fiscal withdrawal
The magnitude of profits above counterfactual (e.g. 30-40% above) is unsupported by rigorous counterfactual specification
Fiscal-default elasticity is uncalibrated — directionally certain, quantitatively imprecise
Political durability of 6-7% deficits is unpredictable
Does not account for external financing constraints via dollar cycle
Transmission lags at each chain link are unspecified
Cross-country calibration data has not been applied
DOGE fiscal consolidation as exogenous loop-breaker is unmodeled
Does not account for multiplier effects or second-round transmission timing
